name: Hunter Knob
short_description: A gentle-slope hillside rockhounding site in the Blue Ridge foothills near Hiawassee. Hunt for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in this accessible North Georgia location.
description:
Hunter Knob sits in the northern Georgia mountains within the Blue Ridge physiographic province, at a modest elevation of 1,957 feet. The area is known for exposures of metamorphic rocks typical of the Appalachian highlands, where quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica sheets are commonly found weathering from outcrops. The gentle slope makes this a more relaxed collecting experience compared to steeper mountain sites, though the rocky terrain still requires sturdy footwear and basic rockhounding skills.

This location benefits from the region's rich geological history. During the Alleghenian orogeny (mountain-building event) roughly 300 million years ago, intense pressure and heat transformed sedimentary rocks into metamorphic varieties. The result is abundant pegmatite and quartz vein material that collectors can find in surface fragments and small outcrops. Mica flakes and feldspar crystals weather out naturally, making this a productive spot for specimen collectors.

The Towns County area has a long tradition of informal rockhounding and mineral collecting. While not a commercially developed fee site, the gentle topography and accessible geology make it popular with local collectors and families new to the hobby. Expect to find loose fragments scattered on hillsides and around weathered rock exposures. The site is best visited during cooler months when the North Georgia climate is mild and pleasant for outdoor work.
history: Hunter Knob sits within the Blue Ridge Mountains, formed during the Alleghenian orogeny when ancient oceanic plates collided with North America roughly 300 million years ago. This massive geological event transformed sedimentary rocks into metamorphic varieties and created abundant pegmatite veins rich in quartz, feldspar, and mica that collectors find today.
tips: Search along the gentle hillsides and around weathered rock outcrops where fragments naturally weather out and collect in small depressions. Look for quartz pieces with cloudy or milky white color, flat feldspar sheets showing cleavage planes, and the characteristic sparkle of mica flakes in direct sunlight. A small hand lens is invaluable for identifying feldspar twinning planes and mica layer structure. Work early in the day when the light is best for spotting pale quartz and feldspar specimens against the rocky hillside.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,957 feet with gentle slopes covered in mixed pine and deciduous forest. Rocky, uneven footing typical of Appalachian hillsides. Expect areas of loose rock fragments from natural weathering. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point, but generally moderate hiking effort. Footwear with good ankle support is essential.
best_season: April - May, September - November. Cool temperatures make spring and fall ideal for hiking and collecting. Summer heat and humidity can be oppressive at this elevation. Winter conditions are generally mild but occasional ice on north-facing slopes is possible.
written_directions: From Hiawassee, take GA 76 north toward Union County approximately 8 miles. Look for Forest Service road access or informal pullouts near Hunter Knob on the east side of the highway. Parking is limited to roadside pullouts. Local knowledge or detailed maps are helpful for locating exact access points.
road_conditions: GA 76 is paved. Forest Service roads and pullouts vary from gravel to rough dirt. Most passenger vehicles can access main pullouts.
parking_notes: Limited roadside pullouts along GA 76. No developed parking area. Capacity typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect only surface fragments and weathered pieces. Do not hammer live rock outcrops or remove anchored specimens.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Do not hammer live rock outcrops
Collect surface fragments and weathered specimens only
nearest_services: Hiawassee, GA approximately 8 miles south. Offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slopes and uneven rocky terrain accessible to most mobile people. No wheelchair access due to natural hillside conditions and lack of maintained trails. Sturdy hiking boots essential for stability on loose rock and uneven footing. Moderate walking fitness recommended.

faq.question: What rocks and gems can you find rockhounding at Hunter Knob near Hiawassee, Georgia?
faq.answer: Hunter Knob is known for quartz specimens, feldspar crystals, and mica flakes. You will find these as loose fragments and weathered pieces on the hillsides and around rock outcrops. Quartz typically appears as milky white or cloudy pieces, feldspar as flat sheets with visible cleavage planes, and mica as thin sparkly flakes. This is an ideal location for learning to identify common metamorphic minerals.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Hunter Knob free and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Hunter Knob is free and no permit is required. The site is on national forest land and allows personal use collection under FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 lbs per day and can only collect surface fragments and weathered specimens. No commercial collection or motorized excavation is permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Hunter Knob in North Georgia?
faq.answer: The best seasons are April through May and September through November. These months offer cool, comfortable temperatures ideal for outdoor hiking and collecting. Summer is hot and humid, while winter can bring occasional ice on north-facing slopes. Spring and fall weather makes field work more enjoyable and safer.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Hunter Knob for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Hunter Knob is beginner-friendly and family-appropriate. The gentle slopes and accessible terrain make it suitable for children ages 6 and up. You do not need advanced rockhounding experience to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Sturdy footwear is essential due to loose rock, but no special tools or skills are required beyond a basic hand lens to examine specimens.

faq.question: What tools and gear do you need for rockhounding at Hunter Knob, Georgia?
faq.answer: Essential gear includes sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support for the uneven rocky terrain, a small hand lens or magnifying glass for identifying mica and feldspar cleavage, and a collection bag to carry your finds. A small brush or toothbrush helps clean specimens. No hammer is needed since you collect only weathered surface fragments. Bring plenty of water, sunscreen, and insect repellent for the forested hillside environment.

faq.question: How far is Hunter Knob from Hiawassee and what are nearby amenities?
faq.answer: Hunter Knob is approximately 8 miles north of Hiawassee, Georgia along GA 76. Hiawassee offers gas stations, restaurants, lodging, and supply stores for any last-minute gear needs. Cell service is spotty in the rockhounding area, so plan ahead and carry printed maps or detailed GPS coordinates. The remote setting makes early departure from town advisable to maximize daylight collecting time.
